shock.adult_dose: Balanced Salt Solution (e.g., Ringer's Lactate): 500ml IV bolus
shock.notes_updates: Balanced Salt Solutions preferred over 0.9% Saline for septic shock to prevent hyperchloraemic acidosis.
0.9% Saline remains primary for hypovolaemia due to blood loss/dehydration.

inotropes.notes_updates: First-line vasopressor for septic shock. No longer restricted as 'Section 21'.
Second-line for septic shock if MAP targets not met with Noradrenaline.
Indicated for septic shock with cardiac dysfunction (persistent hypoperfusion despite fluids/vasopressors).
Largely superseded by Noradrenaline for shock.

resuscitation_medication.paediatric_dose: 10ug/kg IV OR 0.1ml/kg of 1:10 000 (1mg adrenaline/10ml normal saline)

resuscitation_medication.adult_dose: 300mg IV bolus over 3min (unstable pt); 150mg IV bolus over 3min (stable pt)

sedation.notes: Preferred for asthma/bronchospasm or trauma intubation

sedation.notes_updates: Avoid routine deep sedation; prioritize pain control and neuro-psychological support.
Adjust dose in obesity. Caution: Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS).
Preferred for asthma/bronchospasm or trauma intubation.

seizures.adult_dose: 10mg IM or buccal (if no IV access)

seizures.notes_updates: Safer alternative for women of child-bearing potential.

analgesia.notes_updates: Encouraged as part of multimodal analgesia.
Avoid if renal impairment or GI bleeding

neuroprotective.notes_updates: Monitor osmolar gap; avoid if hypotensive
Alternative to mannitol for raised ICP

broad_spectrum_antibiotics.notes_updates: Extended infusion: 3-4hr. High dose for meningitis/CNS

hyperkalaemia_management.notes: Drives K+ into cells. Monitor glucose q15min
Removes K+ from gut. Slow onset (several hours)

diuretics.notes: Monitor electrolytes. Ototoxicity at high doses

vitamins.notes: Give BEFORE glucose in malnourished/alcoholic patients

protocols.adult_dose: NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml 5% Dextrose IV over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 16hrs | NAC SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L 5% Dextrose IV over 10hrs
protocols.notes: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | Use Paracetamol Treatment Nomogram to establish need for antidote | Do NOT withhold even if ingestion was 48-72hrs prior | Activated charcoal 50g PO if presentation <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only useful if >4hrs post-ingestion | Start treatment preferably <8hrs post-ingestion | If in doubt, give NAC
protocols.notes_updates: Toxic dose: 150mg/kg or >10g in adults | NAC Old Regimen: 150mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 1hr, then 50mg/kg in 500ml over 4hrs, then 100mg/kg in 1L over 16hrs | SNAP Protocol: 100mg/kg in 200ml 5% Dextrose IV over 2hrs, then 200mg/kg in 1L over 10hrs | Give activated charcoal 50g PO if <1hr post-ingestion | Paracetamol levels only if >4hrs post-ingestion | Do NOT withhold NAC even if 48-72hrs post-ingestion | Cautions: anaphylaxis, vomiting | High-risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction, elderly | Pregnancy: paracetamol crosses placenta, administer NAC as above

management_steps.details: Monitor glucose, quantify amount ingested and co-ingestants, establish timeframe
If presentation <1 hour from ingestion. Not via NGT.
Look for signs of co-ingestants e.g. prolonged QT
Blood gas, U&E, salicylate level, paracetamol level (>4hrs post-ingestion)
Use nomogram. If high dosage suspected, give antidote. Start <8hrs post-ingestion.
Internal Medicine referral

warnings: Do NOT withhold NAC even if ingestion was 48-72hrs prior to presentation
Separate IV infusion as may be inactivated by other medications
Anaphylaxis and vomiting are less common with SNAP Protocol
High-risk patients: hypotensive, pre-existing liver dysfunction (e.g. alcoholic), elderly

protocols.adult_dose: Naloxone 0.4-4mg IV q2-3min up to 10mg total | Infusion: 0.04-0.16mg/kg/hr IV
protocols.notes: Reversal agent with shorter half-life than most opioids (~1 hour) | Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support | Naloxone 0.04-2mg IV boluses per toxidrome guide | Consider naloxone infusion if recurrent sedation | Airway protection paramount
protocols.notes_updates: Naloxone 0.4-4mg IV q2-3min up to 10mg total | Infusion: 0.04-0.16mg/kg/hr | Shorter half-life than most opioids (~1 hour) - may require redosing | Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support | If possible opioid overdose per BLS algorithm, administer naloxone per protocol | Toxidrome: sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis/mydriasis | Common agents: opioids, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, Nyaope and other opioid-based illicit substances

clinical_features.presentation: Sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis/mydriasis
clinical_features.common_agents: Opioids, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, Nyaope and other opioid-based illicit substances

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
Mainstay of treatment - ventilatory and cardiovascular support
0.4-4mg IV q2-3min up to 10mg. Infusion 0.04-0.16mg/kg/hr if recurrent sedation.
Especially benzodiazepines and alcohol
ICU/High care for ventilatory support if needed

warnings: Naloxone has shorter half-life than most opioids (~1 hour) - redosing often required
Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support, not just naloxone
Watch for recurrent sedation after naloxone wears off

protocols.adult_dose: Flumazenil: 0.2mg IV over 15sec, then 0.1mg every 60sec (max 1mg) | Caution: Generally AVOID Flumazenil
protocols.notes: AVOID Flumazenil - can precipitate withdrawal/seizures | Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support | Supportive care is primary treatment | Toxidrome features: sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis/mydriasis
protocols.notes_updates: AVOID Flumazenil - can precipitate withdrawal/seizures in chronic users and co-ingestions | Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support | Flumazenil if used: 0.2mg IV over 15sec, then 0.1mg every 60sec to max 1mg | Toxidrome: sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression | Common agents: benzodiazepines, barbiturates | Supportive care with airway protection is primary treatment

clinical_features.presentation: Sedation, coma, respiratory and cardiovascular depression, miosis/mydriasis

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
Mainstay of treatment - airway protection and ventilation
Fluids, airway protection, cardiovascular support
Single agent ingestion, no chronic use, no seizure risk. 0.2mg IV over 15sec then 0.1mg q60s to max 1mg.
ICU/High care if ventilatory support required

warnings: AVOID Flumazenil in chronic benzodiazepine users - can precipitate withdrawal/seizures
AVOID Flumazenil in mixed overdoses (especially with pro-convulsant agents)
Mainstay of treatment is ventilatory and cardiovascular support

protocols.adult_dose: Atropine: 0.04-0.05mg/kg IV (3-5mg adult), double every 5min until atropinised | Infusion: 200mg Atropine in 200ml 0.9% NaCl (1mg/ml), 10-20% of total dose/hr | Pralidoxime: 1-2g IV
protocols.notes: Decontamination: remove clothing, wash patient with soap and water | Universal precautions mandatory | Avoid succinylcholine (causes prolonged paralysis) | Atropine treats muscarinic symptoms only | May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely, insert urine catheter | Endpoint = clearing of respiratory secretions (atropinisation), NOT tachycardia or pupil dilation | Seizures: benzodiazepines only (Clonazepam, Diazepam, Lorazepam) - NO anticonvulsants | Monitor and correct electrolytes. Consider MgSO4 4g/day | Weakness (nicotinic): intubation and supportive management only way to control
protocols.notes_updates: Muscarinic symptoms ('DUMBELS'): Diaphoresis, Diarrhoea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchorrhoea, Bronchospasm, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation | Nicotinic: Fasciculations, Weakness, Hypertension, Tachycardia, Mydriasis | CNS: Anxiety, Ataxia, Restlessness, Confusion, Convulsions, Coma | Decontamination: remove clothing, wash with soap and water | Atropine STAT: 0.04-0.05mg/kg IV (3-5mg adult), double every 5min until atropinised ('escalating atropinisation') | Atropine Infusion: 200mg in 200ml 0.9% NaCl, 10-20% of total atropinising dose/hr | Endpoint: clearing of respiratory secretions (NOT tachycardia or pupil dilation) | Pralidoxime: 1-2g IV | Avoid succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis) | Seizures: benzodiazepines ONLY - no anticonvulsants | Monitor electrolytes, consider MgSO4 4g/day

clinical_features.muscarinic_dumbels: Diaphoresis, Diarrhoea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchorrhoea, Bronchospasm, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation
clinical_features.cns: Anxiety, Ataxia, Restlessness, Confusion, Convulsions, Coma

management_steps.details: Remove clothing, wash patient with soap and water
General management principles of poisoned patient
0.04-0.05mg/kg IV (3-5mg adult), double every 5min until atropinised. Can be given IM if no IV access (NB Section 21 form for 100mg amps)
200mg Atropine in 200ml 0.9% NaCl (1mg/ml). 10-20% of total dose/hr once atropinised.
1-2g IV
Benzodiazepines only (Clonazepam, Diazepam, Lorazepam). NO anticonvulsants.
Consider MgSO4 4g/day
If unable to lift head for 10s or squeeze hand, intubate and support
Internal Medicine consultation

warnings: Atropine only treats muscarinic symptoms - does NOT treat nicotinic symptoms
May cause anticholinergic toxidrome at high doses - monitor closely, insert urine catheter
Avoid succinylcholine if intubation required (causes prolonged paralysis)
100mg atropine amps in resus are Section 21 - form required
Endpoint = clearing of respiratory secretions (atropinisation), NOT tachycardia or pupil dilation

protocols.adult_dose: Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus then 1-5mg/hr infusion | HIET: Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV stat then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr infusion | Calcium chloride: 10ml 10% over 10min via large bore IV/central line (target Ca 2mmol) | Consider Intralipid emulsion
protocols.notes: Clinical features: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia, acidosis, CNS depression | ECG: Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia | Propranolol also acts as Na channel blocker (TCA-like features) | Sotalol blocks K channels (QT prolongation, Torsades) | Enhanced elimination: Activated charcoal 50g via NGT | Haemodynamic support: Fluid bolus, high-dose inotropes | HIET protocol: Start with 50ml 50% dextrose bolus, Actrapid 1 IU/kg load, infusion 0.5 IU/kg/hr titrate up to 10 IU/kg/hr, dextrose maintenance 25g/hr via central line. Monitor HGT q20min for 1st hour then q1hr. Monitor potassium - replace if <2.5. | Therapeutic endpoints: EF>50%, SBP>90mmHg, HR>60bpm, resolution of acidaemia, euglycaemia, UO 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms, improved GCS | Consider ECMO if no response to HIET
protocols.notes_updates: Clinical features: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia (BB) or hyperglycaemia (CCB), acidosis, CNS depression | ECG: Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia | Propranolol: also Na channel blocker - TCA-like features | Sotalol: blocks K channels - QT prolongation, Torsades | Enhanced elimination: Activated charcoal 50g via NGT | Haemodynamic support: Fluid bolus, high-dose inotropes | Glucagon: 5-10mg IV bolus then 1-5mg/hr | HIET: Actrapid 1 IU/kg IV load, then 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr. Dextrose 25g/hr maintenance. Monitor HGT q20min then q1hr. Replace K+ if <2.5 | Calcium chloride: 10ml 10% over 10min (target Ca 2mmol) | Intralipid emulsion - specialist consultation | ECMO if no response to HIET | Therapeutic endpoints: EF>50%, SBP>90, HR>60, pH normalised, euglycaemia, UO 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms

clinical_features.clinical: Hypotension, bradycardia, hypoglycaemia (BB) or hyperglycaemia (CCB), acidosis, CNS depression
clinical_features.ecg: Sinus bradycardia, AV blocks, junctional bradycardia, ventricular bradycardia
clinical_features.special_notes: Propranolol also Na channel blocker (TCA-like features); Sotalol blocks K channels (QT prolongation, Torsades)

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
Enhanced elimination
Fluid bolus 20ml/kg, high-dose inotropes as required
10ml 10% over 10min via large bore IV or central line (target Ca 2mmol)
50ml 50% dextrose bolus, Actrapid 1 IU/kg load, 0.5-10 IU/kg/hr infusion, dextrose 25g/hr maintenance
5-10mg IV bolus then 1-5mg/hr infusion
Specialist consultation
EF>50%, SBP>90, HR>60, pH normalised, euglycaemia, UO 1-2ml/kg/hr, QRS<120ms
If no response to HIET - specialist consultation

warnings: Propranolol also acts as Na channel blocker - TCA-like features of OD
Sotalol blocks myocardial K channels - QT prolongation and Torsades
Beta blocker OD may lead to profound CNS depression - airway support needed
Monitor potassium during HIET - replace if <2.5 mmol/L
Consider ECMO if no response to HIET

protocols.adult_dose: Sodium Bicarbonate: 1-2mEq/kg (1-2ml/kg 8.4% or 2-4ml/kg 4.2%) IV bolus, repeat every 5min until BP improves and QRS narrows | Hypertonic saline 3% 100ml boluses if Sodabic unavailable | Lignocaine: 1.5mg/kg IV for arrhythmias once pH >7.5 | Magnesium Sulphate for QT prolongation
protocols.notes: Significant overdose >10mg/kg | ECG features: Na channel blocking, sinus tachycardia, prolonged PR, QRS>100ms (predictive of seizures), QRS>160ms (predictive of VT), prolonged QT, RBBB, right axis deviation, terminal R wave >3mm in aVR, R/S ratio >0.7 in aVR | MAINSTAY: Sodium bicarbonate and hyperventilation | Indications for Sodabic: pH<7.1, QRS>100ms, arrhythmias, seizures, hypotension | Intubate early for airway protection and hyperventilation to pH 7.5-7.55 | Activated charcoal 1g/kg via NGT once airway protected (even if >1hr post-ingestion), consider MDAC in severe cases | Benzodiazepines for seizures - AVOID anti-epileptic drugs | 3rd line: Lignocaine 1.5mg/kg IV once pH>7.5 | Consider Intralipid in consultation with GIT/ICU | Contraindicated: Type 1a antiarrhythmics, Amiodarone, beta-blockers
protocols.notes_updates: Significant overdose >10mg/kg | ECG: Na channel blocker, sinus tachycardia, PR prolongation, QRS>100ms (predicts seizures), QRS>160ms (predicts VT), QT prolongation, RBBB, right axis deviation, terminal R wave >3mm in aVR, R/S >0.7 in aVR | MAINSTAY: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2mEq/kg IV bolus + hyperventilation | Indications for Sodabic: pH<7.1, QRS>100ms, arrhythmias, seizures, hypotension | Repeat Sodabic every 5min until BP improves and QRS narrows | Hyperventilate to pH 7.5-7.55 | Hypertonic saline 3% 100ml boluses if Sodabic unavailable | Intubate early - BEWARE worsening acidosis, minimise apnoeic period | Charcoal 1g/kg via NGT once airway protected (even if >1hr), consider MDAC | Seizures: benzodiazepines, AVOID anti-epileptic drugs | Lignocaine 1.5mg/kg IV for arrhythmias once pH>7.5 | MgSO4 for QT prolongation | Intralipid - consult GIT/ICU | Contraindicated: Type 1a antiarrhythmics, Amiodarone, beta-blockers

clinical_features.presentation: Sedation/coma/seizures, metabolic acidosis, broad complex tachydysrhythmias, hypotension, anticholinergic syndrome
clinical_features.ecg_features: Na channel blockade, sinus tachycardia, prolonged PR, QRS>100ms, prolonged QT, RBBB, right axis deviation, terminal R wave >3mm in aVR

management_steps.details: Even if patient initially appears well
2 IV lines, monitors, HGT, VBG, urine beta-HCG, ECG
1-2mEq/kg IV bolus. Repeat every 5min until BP improves and QRS narrows.
Intubate early, hyperventilate to pH 7.5-7.55. Beware worsening acidosis.
1g/kg via NGT once airway protected. Consider MDAC in severe cases.
Benzodiazepines. AVOID anti-epileptic drugs. Sodabic is 1st line for seizures.
Continue Sodabic boluses until rhythm normalises. Lignocaine 1.5mg/kg IV once pH>7.5.
Magnesium Sulphate
In consultation with GIT/ICU
Refractory cases

warnings: QRS >100ms predictive of seizures; QRS >160ms predictive of ventricular arrhythmias
Cardioversion unlikely to be successful for arrhythmias - continue Sodabic until rhythm normalises
Contraindicated drugs: Type 1a antiarrhythmics, Amiodarone, beta-blockers
Avoid intubation if possible - associated with high peri-intubation mortality in salicylate OD (different protocol)
Avoid anti-epileptic drugs for seizures - use benzodiazepines and Sodabic

protocols.adult_dose: Activated charcoal: 50g if >150mg/kg ingested (up to 8hrs post-ingestion) | Urinary alkalinisation: Sodium bicarbonate 1ml/kg IV of 8.5% (Sodabic) | Goal serum pH 7.45-7.55, urine pH >7.5 | Haemodialysis for refractory cases
protocols.paediatric_dose: Activated charcoal: 1g/kg | Sodium bicarbonate: weight-based dosing
